title: n8n Training Tutorial
description: Step-by-step guide to mastering n8n workflows

content.value: n8n is an open-source workflow automation platform.
Build workflows by connecting nodes in a visual editor. Each node performs an action (call API, transform data, send message), and data flows between nodes as JSON.

Key Features

  • Data = JSON — Every node receives and returns JSON objects
  • Open-source & Self-host — Run on your server, full data control
  • No-code + Code — Visual editor or write JavaScript/Python
  • 400+ Integrations — Gmail, Slack, HTTP, databases, and more
A workflow is a sequence of connected nodes that process data.

• Trigger starts execution
  • Data flows through connections
  • Each node transforms data
  • Output

content.alt: Hello World workflow in n8n visual editor showing two connected nodes

content.value: Nodes are the building blocks of n8n workflows.
Each node has inputs, outputs, and configuration parameters.

Common Node Types

Node TypeKey QuestionExamples
Trigger NodeWhen does the workflow run?Webhook, Schedule, Gmail Trigger, Manual Trigger
Action NodeWhat to do with the data?Google Sheets, Slack, HTTP Request, Gmail Send, Edit Fields
Logic NodeHow does data flow?IF (branching), Merge (combine), Code (JS/Python)

content.alt: Edit Fields node configuration panel in n8n showing parameters and output

content.value:
Node Structure

  • name: Human-readable node name
  • type: Node type identifier (n8n-nodes-base.set)
  • position: Visual placement on canvas [x, y]
  • parameters: Configuration settings
  • id: Unique identifier
Every node passes JSON to the next node.
Connect nodes together and you create a data pipeline.

content.alt: User processing workflow showing data flow through multiple nodes

content.value: The Data Journey: Create Sample Users → Split Into Items (1 array becomes 2 items) → Transform Data (add new fields to each item)
Expressions in Action

3 Rules to Remember

RuleExplanation
1 item = 1 JSON objectEach object is processed separately through the workflow. If you have 100 items, the workflow runs 100 times internally.
Array = batch processingn8n loops through items automatically—no extra code needed. The Split Into Items node converts an array into individual items.
Access with expressionsUse {{ $json.email }} to get "alice@test.com" for the first item. The $json variable always refers to the current item being processed.
Expressions let you access and manipulate data.
Expression Syntax
Wrap JavaScript in {{ }} to access fields, do math, use methods, or build logic.

{{ $json.fieldName }}       // Access a field
{{ $json.age + 5 }}          // Do math
{{ $json.name.toUpperCase() }} // Use JavaScript methods
{{ $json.role === 'admin' ? 'Full Access' : 'Limited' }} // Conditionals
Variable Types

VariableWhat It DoesWhen to Use
$jsonData from the previous nodeMost common—use by default
$('Node Name')Data from any specific nodeWhen you need data from a node that's not directly before
$inputCurrent node's input dataAdvanced—loops, multiple inputs
$now / $todayCurrent date/timeTimestamps, date filtering, logging
Examples

content.alt: Complete workflow showing all 5 nodes with expression outputs in the data table
Add Metadata node detail view showing input data, expression parameters, and output results

content.value: Logic nodes control where data flows.
Route items down different paths based on conditions, then merge back together.
IF Node — Two paths: true or false

// Example conditions:
{{ $json.role === 'admin' }}              // Is user an admin?
{{ $json.age >= 18 }}                     // Is user 18 or older?
{{ $json.price > 100 }}                   // Is price over $100?
{{ $json.status === 'active' }}           // Is status active?
{{ $json.email.includes('@company.com') }} // Is company email?
Switch Node — Multiple paths based on value
Merge Node — Combine multiple paths back into one

Comparison

NodeOutputsUse When
IF2 (true/false)Binary decision — yes or no, include or exclude
SwitchMultipleRoute by value — status, category, type, etc.
Merge1Combine paths back together after branching
Filter1Remove items that don't match condition

content.alt: Complete workflow showing branching, merging, and Gmail integration with 7 nodes
Split Into Items node converting 1 array into 3 separate user items
IF node detail showing condition check and true/false branch outputs
Merge node showing append mode combining 2 admin items and 1 user item into 3 total items
Gmail node showing HTML message template and successful email sends for all 3 users
Actual Gmail inbox showing the User Access Summary Report with all 3 users listed with their access levels and badges

content.value: Reliable workflows handle errors gracefully.
Use Continue on Fail, Error Triggers, and validation to catch failures and provide visibility.

Error Types

Error TypeExampleSolution
API FailureExternal service returns 500 errorRetry logic, fallback data source
Missing DataExpected field is undefinedDefault values, validation checks
Invalid InputEmail format is incorrectValidation before processing
TimeoutRequest takes too longIncrease timeout, optimize query
Error Trigger Node — Runs when any node fails
Continue on Fail — Node setting to prevent failure from stopping workflow
Validation with IF Node

Debugging Techniques

TechniqueHow To
Inspect Node OutputClick node → View output tab to see actual data
Pin DataRight-click node → Pin data to test with sample data
Execute Single NodeClick node → Execute node to test in isolation
Check Execution LogView execution history to see what went wrong
Use Edit Fields to LogAdd debug fields to see intermediate values
Common Pattern

// Pattern: Validate → Try → Catch → Notify

1. IF Node: Validate input
   ├─ Valid → Continue to main workflow
   └─ Invalid → Send to error handler

2. Enable "Continue On Fail" on risky nodes
   - API calls
   - External integrations
   - File operations

3. Error Trigger workflow:
   - Log error details
   - Send notification (Slack/Email)
   - Store failed items for retry

4. IF Node: Check for errors
   ├─ Success → Continue
   └─ Failed → Fallback action or skip

content.alt: Successful API call showing data flowing through the true branch with user data displayed in output table
Failed API call showing error object flowing through the false branch with error details
Gmail inbox showing the automated error alert email with error details and timestamp

content.value:
Best Practices

  • Fail fast — Validate input early, before expensive operations
  • Be specific — Check for exact error conditions rather than catching all errors
  • Provide context — Include useful info in error messages (execution ID, failed node, input data)
  • Test failure paths — Don't just test the happy path; simulate failures
  • Monitor & alert — Set up Error Trigger workflows for critical workflows
AI APIs let you analyze, generate, and transform data using LLMs.
Send text in → Get intelligent response back.

AI Providers

ProviderModelsBest For
OpenAIGPT-4, GPT-3.5General purpose, JSON mode, function calling
AnthropicClaude 4Long context, analysis, reasoning
GoogleGeminiMultimodal (text + images), fast
Open SourceLlama, MistralSelf-hosted, cost control
Text Generation

Parameters

ParameterWhat It Does
ModelWhich AI to use (gpt-4 = smart but slow, gpt-3.5 = fast but cheaper)
TemperatureCreativity (0 = consistent, 1 = creative). Use 0 for data extraction, 0.7 for writing.
Max TokensResponse length limit. 1 token ≈ 0.75 words. Limits cost.
System MessageSets AI behavior ("You are a helpful customer service agent")
Chat vs Completion
Response Handling

content.alt: Complete AI email responder workflow with 5 nodes: Manual Trigger, Sample Email, Analyze Sentiment, Generate Response, and Format Output
Analyze Sentiment node showing OpenAI configuration with prompt to classify email sentiment as positive, negative, or neutral
Generate Response node with system message and user prompt, showing AI-generated professional customer service email response

content.value: Embeddings convert text into numbers (vectors) that capture meaning.
Similar concepts have similar vectors, enabling semantic search beyond keyword matching.
How It Works
Creating Embeddings

content.alt: OpenAI Embeddings API single text request in Insomnia showing input text and embedding response
OpenAI Embeddings API batch request in Insomnia showing array of texts and embedding response

content.value:
Vector Databases

DatabaseBest ForNotes
PineconeProduction, managed serviceFast, scalable, fully managed
WeaviateSelf-hosted, open sourceGraphQL API, hybrid search
QdrantHigh performance, filteringFast, good for metadata filters
Supabase VectorPostgreSQL extensionpgvector, familiar SQL interface

Measuring Similarity with Cosine Distance

Let's see how embeddings capture meaning by comparing two different ways of describing the same login issue and calculating their similarity.

content.alt: Insomnia showing Embedding 1 request with input text: The user is experiencing login issues with two-factor authentication
Insomnia showing Embedding 2 request with input text: The customer cannot sign in because of two-step verification problem
Claude Code showing cosine similarity calculation result: 0.648507 (64.85%) - moderate similarity with semantic overlap

content.value:
Cosine Similarity: 0.648507 (64.85%)

Interpretation: Moderate similarity - The two inputs share semantic overlap despite using completely different words:

  • "user" vs "customer" - different terms for the same concept
  • "login issues" vs "cannot sign in" - same meaning, different phrasing
  • "two-factor authentication" vs "two-step verification" - technical synonyms

This demonstrates how embeddings capture meaning rather than just matching keywords. A traditional keyword search would miss this connection!

content.alt: Qdrant UI showing both embeddings stored as Point 1 and Point 2 with their source metadata and input text, each with 1536-dimension vectors

content.value:
Storing in Vector Database (Qdrant)

After calculating similarity, we can store these embeddings in a vector database for future semantic search:

content.alt: Claude Code showing the execution plan for generating 20 diverse embeddings with expected similarity patterns

content.value:
How We Generated 20 Diverse Embeddings

Using Claude and Insomnia MCP, we created a rich dataset to visualize semantic similarity:

  1. Claude generated diverse sentences across 7 categories:
    • Authentication & Login: 3 texts
    • Payment & Billing: 3 texts
    • Shipping & Delivery: 3 texts
    • Product Features: 3 texts
    • Technical Support: 3 texts
    • Account Management: 3 texts
    • General Inquiries: 2 texts
  2. Insomnia MCP executed OpenAI embedding requests for each text
  3. All vectors saved to Qdrant with metadata (source, input_text, category)

This diverse dataset demonstrates how embeddings capture semantic meaning across different topics and categories.

content.alt: Qdrant HNSW graph showing 20 embeddings as colored dots with connections, demonstrating semantic clustering by category

content.value:
HNSW Graph Visualization

After uploading 20 diverse embeddings to Qdrant, we can visualize them in the HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) graph. Each dot represents one embedding, and the connections show semantic relationships:

  • Colors represent categories: Cyan (shipping, features, general), Orange (auth, payment, tech, account)
  • Distance = Similarity: Closer dots have more similar meanings
  • Connections: Lines show the nearest neighbors in the vector space
  • Selected point (Point 10): "How do I export my data to CSV format" (feature category)

Key Concepts

  • Embedding dimension: Usually 1536 (OpenAI text-embedding-3-small) or 768 (smaller models)
  • Top-k search: Return k most similar results (typically 3-10) based on vector distance
  • HNSW: Graph structure for fast approximate nearest neighbor search, visualizes semantic relationships
  • Vector distance: Cosine distance measures angle between vectors; closer = more similar meaning
Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) combines vector search with LLMs to answer questions using your own data.
Instead of relying on the LLM's training data, you retrieve relevant context from your knowledge base and inject it into the prompt.
How RAG Works

Traditional LLM: "I don't have access to your company data..." ❌

With RAG:
1. Convert question to embedding
2. Find 3 most similar chunks in vector DB
3. Inject chunks into LLM prompt as context
4. LLM answers using YOUR data ✅

Result: Accurate answers from your knowledge base!
Chunking Essentials

Why chunk? Three reasons:

  1. Model token limits - Embedding models have max input size (8191 tokens)
  2. Retrieval precision - Smaller chunks = more accurate semantic matches
  3. Context efficiency - Only inject relevant chunks into LLM, not entire documents

Best Practices:

1. Size: 200-500 words (sweet spot)
   Too small (50 words):   ❌ Loses context
   Too large (2000 words): ❌ Less precise retrieval
   
2. Overlap: 10-20% (prevents boundary issues)
   Example: 1000-character chunks with 200-char overlap
   Chunk 1: [chars 0-1000]
   Chunk 2: [chars 800-1800]    // 200 overlap
   Chunk 3: [chars 1600-2600]   // 200 overlap

3. Boundaries: Split at paragraphs, not mid-sentence
   ❌ "The password must be at least 12 char" | "acters long"
   ✅ Split at natural breaks

4. Content-aware splitting:
   Code:       Function/class boundaries
   Markdown:   ## headings
   Tables:     Keep entire table together
   JSON/YAML:  Complete objects

Key rule: 1 chunk = 1 embedding vector

Example: Building a RAG System with Shakespeare Text

Let's build a complete RAG system using the Tiny Shakespeare dataset. We'll index ~1MB of Shakespeare's works and enable semantic search.
Indexing Workflow
Workflow Steps:

1. 01 - Manual Trigger
   Click to start the workflow

2. 02 - Fetch Source Text (TinyShakespeare)
   GET https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/char-rnn/master/data/tinyshakespeare/input.txt
   Returns: ~1MB plain text

3. 03 - Chunk Text (1000/200 overlap)
   Input: Full text (~1MB)
   Chunk size: 1000 characters
   Overlap: 200 characters (20%)
   Output: ~1200 chunks

4. 04 - Create Embedding Batches (50)
   Input: 1200 chunks
   Batch size: 50 chunks per batch
   Output: ~24 batches
   Why? OpenAI embeddings API accepts multiple inputs

5. 05 - Generate Embeddings (text-embedding-3-small)
   POST https://api.openai.com/v1/embeddings
   Model: text-embedding-3-small
   Input: Array of 50 texts
   Output: Array of 50 embeddings (1536 dimensions each)
   Runs 24 times (one per batch)

6. 06 - Map Embeddings → Qdrant Points
   Combine chunks with their embeddings
   Create point structure for Qdrant
   Format: { id, vector, payload: { chunk_index, text } }

7. 07 - Upsert Points → Qdrant (tinyshakespeare)
   PUT https://[qdrant-url]/collections/tinyshakespeare/points
   Stores 50 points per request
   Runs 24 times (one per batch)
   Total: ~1200 searchable chunks

Result: Shakespeare text fully indexed and ready for semantic search!

content.alt: RAG indexing workflow showing 7 nodes: Manual Trigger, HTTP Request to fetch Shakespeare text, Code to split into chunks, Code to create batches, HTTP Request to OpenAI embeddings, Code to prepare Qdrant points, and HTTP Request to store in Qdrant
Qdrant UI showing tinyshakespeare collection with stored points, displaying Point 1 and Point 2 with their chunk_index and Shakespeare text payloads

content.value:
Stored Points in Qdrant:

• Collection: tinyshakespeare
• Vector size: 1536 dimensions (text-embedding-3-small)
• Distance: Cosine similarity
• Total points: ~1200 chunks
• Payload: chunk_index (number) + text (string)
Query Workflow
Workflow Steps:

1. Webhook - Receive Question
   POST /rag-query
   Body: { "question": "Who is Marcius?" }
   Waits for response before returning

2. HTTP Request - Generate Query Embedding
   POST https://api.openai.com/v1/embeddings
   Model: text-embedding-3-small
   Input: {{ $json.body.question }}
   Output: { data: [{ embedding: [0.12, -0.45, ...] }] }

3. HTTP Request - Vector Search (Qdrant)
   POST https://[qdrant-url]/collections/tinyshakespeare/points/search
   Body:
     vector: {{ $json.data[0].embedding }}
     limit: 5
     with_payload: true
   Returns: Top 5 most similar chunks with their text

4. Code - Build RAG Prompt
   Extract context from search results
   Combine context with original question
   Create structured prompt for LLM

5. OpenAI Chat Model - Generate Answer
   Model: chatgpt-4o-latest
   Input: RAG prompt with context
   Output: Answer based on Shakespeare text

6. Respond to Webhook - Return Response
   Extract answer text from LLM output
   Return as plain text response

Result: User gets accurate answer from indexed Shakespeare text!

content.alt: RAG query workflow showing 6 nodes: Webhook to receive question, HTTP Request to generate embedding, HTTP Request to search Qdrant, Code to build RAG prompt, OpenAI Chat to generate answer, and Respond to Webhook

content.value:
Testing the RAG System

Using Insomnia to send a question about Shakespeare's text:

content.alt: Insomnia showing POST request to RAG query endpoint with question: Who is Marcius?

content.value: Request:
Response:
Marcius is a noble Roman soldier, renowned for his valor and leadership in battle. He is depicted as a courageous warrior who plays a significant role in the conflicts between Rome and its enemies.

How It Works:

  1. Question "Who is Marcius?" → Converted to embedding
  2. Vector search finds 5 most similar Shakespeare text chunks
  3. LLM generates answer using ONLY those chunks
  4. Result: Accurate answer about character from Coriolanus

✅ The system correctly identified Marcius from Shakespeare's play using semantic search, not keyword matching!

Common Patterns

  • Hybrid search: Combine vector similarity + keyword filters (e.g., only search "billing" category)
  • Re-ranking: Use a second model to re-order results for better relevance
  • Citation: Include source metadata in response (article title, URL, page number)
  • Fallback: If similarity score < 0.7, respond "I don't have information about that"
  • Conversational RAG: Include chat history in embedding for context-aware answers

Key Metrics to Monitor

  • Retrieval accuracy: Are the right chunks being found?
  • Answer quality: Are LLM responses accurate and helpful?
  • Latency: Embedding + search + LLM should be < 3 seconds
  • Cost: Track embedding API calls + vector DB queries + LLM tokens
AI Agents combine RAG with conversational memory to create interactive, context-aware assistants.
Building on Step 10's RAG foundation, we'll add conversation tracking so the agent remembers previous questions and builds on context.
What Makes an Agent?

Simple RAG (Step 10):
Question → Vector Search → LLM → Answer
❌ No memory of previous questions
❌ Each query is isolated

Conversational Agent (Step 11):
Question + Session ID → Load History → Vector Search → LLM → Answer
✅ Remembers conversation context
✅ Can reference previous questions
✅ Builds on prior answers
✅ More natural interaction

Example:
User: "Who is Marcius?"
Agent: "Marcius is a noble Roman soldier..."

User: "What are his key characteristics?"  // Agent knows "his" = Marcius
Agent: "Based on our previous discussion about Marcius, his key characteristics include..."
Shakespeare Conversational Agent
Workflow Architecture:

1. Webhook - Receive Question + Session ID
   POST /shakespeare-chat
   Body: { 
     "question": "Who is Marcius?",
     "session_id": "test-1"
   }

2. Prepare Session Data (Code)
   Extract session_id and question from webhook
   Prepare data for next nodes

3. Get Conversation History (Data table)
   Search conversations table
   Filter by session_id = 'test-1'
   Sort by created_at DESC, Limit 10
   Returns: Last 10 messages for this session

4. Generate Query Embedding (HTTP Request)
   POST https://api.openai.com/v1/embeddings
   Input: Current question
   Output: 1536-dimension vector

5. Vector Search (Qdrant)
   POST /collections/tinyshakespeare/points/search
   Search with query embedding
   Find 3 most similar Shakespeare chunks
   Returns: Text excerpts with relevance scores

6. Build RAG Prompt (Code)
   Combine:
   - Chat history (from Data table)
   - RAG results (from Qdrant)
   - Current question
   Create structured prompt for LLM

7. OpenAI Chat Model (Message a model)
   Model: chatgpt-4o-latest
   Temperature: 0.7
   Generate answer using combined context

8. Save User Message (Data table)
   Insert user's question into conversations table
   Includes: session_id, role='user', content, created_at

9. Save Assistant Message (Data table)
   Insert AI's answer into conversations table
   Includes: session_id, role='assistant', content, created_at

10. Respond to Webhook
    Return assistant's answer as text

Result: Context-aware Shakespeare Q&A with persistent memory!

content.alt: Shakespeare Conversational Agent workflow showing 10 nodes in n8n: Webhook, Prepare Session Data, Get Conversation History (Data table), Generate Query Embedding, Vector Search (Qdrant), Build RAG Prompt, OpenAI Chat Model, Save User Message, Save Assistant Message, and Respond to Webhook. The workflow successfully executed with a detailed response about Marcius displayed in the output panel.

content.value: Combining Chat History + RAG Context

Why This Structure Works:

  • RAG context first - Grounds the LLM in factual data
  • Chat history second - Provides conversational context
  • Current question last - Focuses the LLM on the task
  • Clear instructions - Tells LLM how to use the context
Multi-Turn Conversation Example

content.alt: Insomnia showing a follow-up question 'What are his key characteristics?' with session_id 'test-1'. The response begins with 'Based on our previous discussion about Marcius...' demonstrating the agent's conversational memory - it remembers the previous question about who Marcius is and provides context-aware characteristics including bravery, honor, pride, and his complex relationships.

content.value: Context Window Management
LLMs have token limits. Manage conversation history to avoid hitting those limits.
Conversation Memory with Data Tables
We use n8n's built-in Data table feature to store conversation history - perfect for n8n Cloud, no external database needed!

Why session_id?

  • Isolate conversations - Different users or conversation threads remain separate
  • Multi-turn support - Track all messages within a single conversation
  • History retrieval - Load previous messages for context
  • Session management - Enable timeout, cleanup, and rate limiting

Create Conversations Table

In n8n, go to Data → Data tables and create a new table called 'conversations' with these columns:

Table name: conversations

Columns:
- id (Number) - Auto-generated primary key
- session_id (String) - Groups messages by conversation session
- role (String) - Either 'user' or 'assistant'
- content (String) - The actual message text
- createdAt (Date) - Auto-generated timestamp
- updatedAt (Date) - Auto-generated timestamp

Note: id, createdAt, and updatedAt are created automatically.
You only need to add: session_id, role, and content.
The Data table feature in n8n Cloud stores data persistently without needing an external database. Perfect for conversation memory!
Example Data Structure:

id | session_id | role      | content                           | createdAt
---|------------|-----------|-----------------------------------|-------------------
1  | test-1     | user      | Who is Marcius?                   | 2026-02-16T11:10:10Z
2  | test-1     | assistant | Based on the excerpts provided... | 2026-02-16T11:10:15Z
3  | test-1     | user      | What are his key characteristics? | 2026-02-16T11:15:25Z
4  | test-1     | assistant | Based on our previous discussion... | 2026-02-16T11:15:30Z

Note: All rows with session_id='test-1' belong to the same conversation.

Save User & Assistant Messages (2 nodes)

We use two separate Data table Insert nodes to store messages.
Node 8: Save User Message

Operation: Insert
Table: conversations
Data to Send: Define Below for Each Column

Fields to Send:
  session_id: {{ $('Prepare Session Data').first().json.session_id }}
  role: user
  content: {{ $('Prepare Session Data').first().json.question }}
Node 9: Save Assistant Message

Operation: Insert
Table: conversations
Data to Send: Define Below for Each Column

Fields to Send:
  session_id: {{ $('Prepare Session Data').first().json.session_id }}
  role: assistant
  content: {{ $('OpenAI Chat Model').first().json.content[0].text }}

Important:

  • Two separate nodes ensure both messages are stored
  • createdAt timestamps are auto-generated by Data table
  • Use expressions to reference data from previous nodes
  • For "Message a model" node: use .json.content[0].text to get the response

Viewing Conversation History in Data Tables

After the workflow executes, you can view all stored conversations in the n8n Data tables UI.

content.alt: n8n Data tables view showing the conversations table with 15 rows of stored messages. All entries have session_id='test-1', alternating between role='user' and role='assistant'. The content column shows multi-turn conversations about Marcius, including questions like 'Who is Marcius?' and 'What are his key characteristics?' with detailed AI responses. Each row has auto-generated id, createdAt, and updatedAt timestamps.

content.value:
How to Access:

  1. In n8n, click Data in the left sidebar
  2. Click Data tables
  3. Select the conversations table
  4. View all stored messages with full conversation history

What You'll See:

  • session_id - All messages with same session_id are part of one conversation
  • role - Alternates between 'user' (questions) and 'assistant' (answers)
  • content - Full message text, truncated in table view (click row to see full text)
  • createdAt - Timestamp showing when each message was stored
  • Multi-turn conversations - Notice how answers reference previous questions (e.g., "Based on our previous discussion...")

Verifying Memory Works:

Look for assistant responses that start with phrases like:

  • "Based on our previous discussion about Marcius..."
  • "As mentioned earlier..."
  • "Following up on your question about..."

These phrases prove the agent is using conversation history from the Data table! ✅
Session Management Best Practices
Common Patterns

Key Takeaways

  • Conversational memory enables multi-turn interactions
  • n8n Data table provides built-in, persistent storage (no external database needed)
  • Context management prevents token limit issues
  • RAG + Memory = powerful, context-aware agents
  • Session isolation keeps conversations separate
  • Structured prompts combine history + knowledge effectively
  • Cloud-friendly - works perfectly in n8n Cloud without infrastructure setup
Next Steps

Once you have this conversational foundation, you can extend the agent with:

  • Conversation summarization - Condense long histories to manage token limits
  • Multi-turn clarification - Ask follow-up questions when user intent is unclear
  • Tool calling - Let the agent decide when to use RAG vs other capabilities
  • Multiple knowledge bases - Search different collections based on topic
  • Topic tracking - Detect when conversation shifts to new subjects
  • Sentiment analysis - Adjust tone based on user mood
  • Multi-language support - Translate questions and answers
  • Conversational UI - Build a chat interface for better user experience
Transform your conversational agent into a user-friendly chat interface with multi-language support.
Building on Step 11's conversational memory foundation, we'll add a native chat UI that makes interaction natural and intuitive - no more API testing tools required!
What's New in Step 12?

Step 11: Conversational Agent (Webhook-based)
POST /shakespeare-chat
{ "question": "Who is Marcius?", "session_id": "test-1" }
✅ Memory works
❌ Requires API client (Insomnia/Postman)
❌ Not user-friendly for non-technical users

Step 12: Chat UI (Native n8n Chat)
[Chat Interface] "Who is Marcius?" → Send
✅ Memory works
✅ Visual chat interface
✅ Multi-language support (Vietnamese, English, etc.)
✅ Real-time conversation experience
✅ No API knowledge needed

Key Improvements:
1. Replace Webhook trigger with "When chat message received"
2. Replace "Respond to Webhook" with "Send message back to chat"
3. Automatic session management (no manual session_id)
4. Multi-language conversation support
Chat UI Workflow Architecture
Workflow Steps:

1. When chat message received (Chat Trigger)
   Type: @n8n/n8n-nodes-langchain.chatTrigger
   Configuration:
     - public: true (makes chat accessible)
     - initialMessages: "👋 Hello! I'm your Shakespeare expert..."
   Provides: chatInput (user message), sessionId (auto-generated)
   No webhook URL needed!

2. Prepare Session Data (Code)
   Extract session ID and chat input from trigger:
   const chatMessage = $input.first().json;
   session_id = chatMessage.sessionId || 'default';
   question = chatMessage.chatInput || '';

3. Get Conversation History (Data table)
   Operation: Get
   Filter by session_id
   Limit: 10 (last 10 messages)
   alwaysOutputData: true

4. Generate Query Embedding (HTTP Request)
   POST https://api.openai.com/v1/embeddings
   Model: text-embedding-3-small
   Input: {{ $('02 - Prepare Session Data').first().json.question }}
   Output: 1536-dimension vector

5. Vector Search (Qdrant)
   POST /collections/tinyshakespeare/points/search
   Body: {
     "vector": $json.data[0].embedding,
     "limit": 3,
     "with_payload": true
   }

6. Build RAG Prompt (Code)
   Extract chat history: historyNode.reverse().map(...)
   Extract RAG context: searchResults.map(item => item.payload.text)
   Build prompt combining both contexts
   Note: chatgpt-4o-latest handles multi-language naturally!

7. OpenAI Chat Model
   Type: @n8n/n8n-nodes-langchain.openAi
   Model: chatgpt-4o-latest
   Input: {{ $('06 - Build RAG Prompt').first().json.prompt }}

8. Save User Message (Data table)
   Operation: Insert
   Columns:
     - session_id: {{ $('06 - Build RAG Prompt').first().json.session_id }}
     - role: user
     - content: {{ $('06 - Build RAG Prompt').first().json.question }}

9. Save Assistant Message (Data table)
   Operation: Insert
   Columns:
     - session_id: {{ $('02 - Prepare Session Data').first().json.session_id }}
     - role: assistant
     - content: {{ $('07 - OpenAI Chat Model').first().json.output[0].content[0].text }}

10. Respond to Webhook
    Returns the assistant's answer to the chat UI
    The chat trigger automatically displays the response

Result: Beautiful chat interface with multi-language support!

content.alt: Shakespeare Conversation Agent with Chat UI workflow in n8n showing the chat interface on the left with a Vietnamese conversation 'Ai là Aufidius?' (Who is Aufidius?), and the workflow with 10 nodes: When chat message received, Prepare Session Data, Get Conversation History, Generate Query Embedding, Vector Search (Qdrant), Build RAG Prompt, OpenAI Chat Model, Save User Message, Save Assistant Message, and Respond to Webhook. The output panel shows the Vietnamese response about Aufidius being an enemy of the Volsci and Caius Marcius.

content.value:
How Multi-Language Works

The beauty of embeddings + LLMs: language translation happens naturally!

// Vietnamese Question
User: "Ai là Aufidius?"
       ↓ Embedding (captures semantic meaning)
       [0.23, -0.45, 0.78, ...]
       ↓ Vector Search (finds English Shakespeare text)
       "Aufidius, he is a lion that I am proud to hunt."
       ↓ LLM Prompt (with instruction)
       "Answer in the same language as the question"
       ↓ Vietnamese Response
Assistant: "Dựa trên các đoạn trích từ vở kịch của Shakespeare, 
**Aufidius** là một nhân vật trong vở kịch *Coriolanus* của 
Shakespeare, **Rome** là thành bang nổi diện ra phần lớn những 
xung đột..."

// English Question
User: "Who is Aufidius?"
       ↓ Embedding (same semantic meaning!)
       [0.22, -0.44, 0.79, ...] // Very similar to Vietnamese
       ↓ Vector Search (same chunks found)
       ↓ English Response
Assistant: "Based on the excerpts from Shakespeare's play, 
Aufidius is a character in Shakespeare's *Coriolanus*, serving 
as the primary antagonist and a general of the Volscian army..."

// Key Points:
1. Embeddings capture MEANING, not language
2. Similar meanings → similar vectors (regardless of language)
3. RAG finds relevant English text
4. LLM translates naturally when instructed
5. Works for ANY language (Vietnamese, Spanish, French, etc.)

Build RAG Prompt Implementation

The actual prompt from the workflow is simple and relies on chatgpt-4o-latest's natural multi-language capabilities:
Multi-Language Conversation in Action

content.alt: Shakespeare Chat UI showing a Vietnamese conversation. The chat panel on the left displays the question 'Mối quan hệ của họ như thế nào?' (What is their relationship like?). The workflow is executing with the '06 - Build RAG Prompt' node highlighted. The output panel on the right shows the RAG prompt combining Shakespeare excerpts, conversation history, and the Vietnamese question with session ID 0e450a91286b494098042cc78d2955fb.

content.value:
What's Happening:

• Chat Interface (Left): 
  User asks in Vietnamese: "Mối quan hệ của họ như thế nào?" 
  (What is their relationship like?)

• Workflow (Center): 
  The workflow executes all 10 nodes automatically, 
  building the RAG prompt with context

• Output (Right): 
  Shows the prompt combining Shakespeare excerpts, 
  conversation history, and the Vietnamese question

• Result: 
  The agent responds in Vietnamese with context from 
  previous conversation about Coriolanus and Aufidius

✅ Multi-language support works seamlessly
✅ Conversation history is preserved
✅ RAG retrieves relevant Shakespeare text regardless of input language
Key Takeaways

• Chat UI makes conversational AI accessible to everyone, not just developers
  • n8n's chat trigger handles session management automatically
  • Multi-language support is built-in with modern LLMs - just add prompt instructions
  • Same backend (Data table + RAG + memory) works for both Chat UI and Webhook
  • Embeddings capture meaning across languages - Vietnamese question finds English text
  • No extra infrastructure needed - everything runs in n8n Cloud
  • Chat UI is perfect for demos, testing, and user-facing applications
